message.content: We should add an `intruction` in the document system, telling users what `ghr` do and how does it do it

addedBlocks:
## plugin:context7:context7
Use this server to fetch current documentation whenever the user asks about a library, framework, SDK, API, CLI tool, or cloud service -- even well-known ones like React, Next.js, Prisma, Express, Tailwind, Django, or Spring Boot. This includes API syntax, configuration, version migration, library-specific debugging, setup instructions, and CLI tool usage. Use even when you think you know the answer -- your training data may not reflect recent changes. Prefer this over web search for library docs.

Do not use for: refactoring, writing scripts from scratch, debugging business logic, code review, or general programming concepts.

input.prompt:
Explore this repository to understand:
1. What is "ghr" - what does it do?

content.text:
Now I have a comprehensive understanding of the repository. Here is my report:

---

## What is "ghr"?

**ghr** is a CLI tool for managing self-hosted [GitHub Actions](https://docs.github.com/en/actions) runners across multiple machines from a single control host (typically your laptop). It connects to machines over **SSH** (or runs locally with `addr: local`), installs and starts runners, and provides a **terminal UI (TUI)** for day-to-day operations.

**Key Features:**
- Unified commands (`setup`, `up`, `down`, `status`, `logs`, `service`, ...) work across **Linux**, **macOS**, and **Windows** runners
- Declarative YAML configuration for hosts and runners; secrets stored in `~/.ghr/env`
- Docker or native runners per row, with sensible OS defaults
- Multi-host management from a single config
- Interactive TUI dashboard (`ghr` or `ghr dashboard` on a TTY)

**Repository:** [github.com/an-lee/ghr](https://github.com/an-lee/ghr)  
**Go module:** `github.com/an-lee/ghr`

---

## Documentation System

The project uses **Hugo** with the **Hugo Book theme** to generate static documentation site published to GitHub Pages at `https://an-lee.github.io/ghr/`.

**Documentation build:**
- Source: `docs/content/` (Markdown files)
- Config: `docs/hugo.toml`
- Theme: `hugo-book` (cloned into `docs/themes/book`)

---

## Documentation Structure

```
docs/
├── hugo.toml                          # Hugo site configuration
└── content/
    ├── _index.md                      # Home / landing page
    ├── installation.md                # Installation instructions
    ├── github-pat.md                  # GitHub PAT setup guide
    ├── configuration.md               # YAML configuration reference
    ├── commands.md                    # CLI commands reference
    ├── architecture.md                # Architecture overview
    ├── host-setup.md                  # Host setup guide
    ├── guides/                        # HOW-TO guides (collapsible section)
    │   ├── _index.md                  # Guides section index
    │   ├── local-host.md              # Running on local host
    │   ├── linux-on-macos.md          # Linux runners on macOS
    │   ├── linux-on-windows.md        # Linux runners on Windows
    │   ├── common-tasks.md             # Common tasks guide
    │   └── workflows.md               # Workflow integration guide
    └── reference/                     # Technical reference (collapsible section)
        ├── _index.md                  # Reference section index
        └── file-structure.md          # Repository file structure reference
```

---

## File Path Summary

**Root documentation:**
- `/home/an-lee/projects/ghr/README.md` - Project README
- `/home/an-lee/projects/ghr/docs/content/_index.md` - Documentation home page
- `/home/an-lee/projects/ghr/docs/hugo.toml` - Hugo site configuration

**Documentation content files:**
- `/home/an-lee/projects/ghr/docs/content/installation.md`
- `/home/an-lee/projects/ghr/docs/content/github-pat.md`
- `/home/an-lee/projects/ghr/docs/content/configuration.md`
- `/home/an-lee/projects/ghr/docs/content/commands.md`
- `/home/an-lee/projects/ghr/docs/content/architecture.md`
- `/home/an-lee/projects/ghr/docs/content/host-setup.md`

**Guides section (`/home/an-lee/projects/ghr/docs/content/guides/`):**
- `/home/an-lee/projects/ghr/docs/content/guides/_index.md`
- `/home/an-lee/projects/ghr/docs/content/guides/local-host.md`
- `/home/an-lee/projects/ghr/docs/content/guides/linux-on-macos.md`
- `/home/an-lee/projects/ghr/docs/content/guides/linux-on-windows.md`
- `/home/an-lee/projects/ghr/docs/content/guides/common-tasks.md`
- `/home/an-lee/projects/ghr/docs/content/guides/workflows.md`

**Reference section (`/home/an-lee/projects/ghr/docs/content/reference/`):**
- `/home/an-lee/projects/ghr/docs/content/reference/_index.md`
- `/home/an-lee/projects/ghr/docs/content/reference/file-structure.md`

2	title: "ghr"
3	weight: 1
4	---
5	
6	# ghr
7	
8	**ghr** is a CLI tool to manage self-hosted [GitHub Actions](https://docs.github.com/en/actions) runners across multiple machines from a single control host—typically your laptop—using SSH or local execution.
9	
10	## Highlights
11	
12	- **Unified commands** — `up`, `down`, `status`, `setup`, and more work for Linux, macOS, and Windows runners.
13	- **SSH and local** — Manage remote hosts over SSH, or use `addr: local` to run runners on the same machine as the CLI.
14	- **Declarative config** — One YAML file lists hosts and runners.
15	- **Multi-host** — Operate any number of runner machines (desktops, Mac minis, VPS).
16	- **Docker and native** — Sensible defaults per OS; override with `mode: docker` or `mode: native` where supported.
17	- **Interactive dashboard** — Run `ghr` on a TTY for a full TUI (same as `ghr dashboard`).
18	
19	## Architecture (overview)
20	
21	```text
22	Your laptop (control plane)
23	  └── ghr CLI
24	        ├── local  → This machine (native/Docker runners)
25	        ├── SSH → Mac mini (native and/or Docker runners)
26	        ├── SSH → Windows PC (native + Docker runners)
27	        └── SSH → VPS (Docker runners)
28	```
29	
30	The CLI reads your YAML config, connects to each host over SSH (or runs locally for `addr: local`), and runs the right commands to install, start, and stop runner processes. Only the machine where you run **ghr** needs the `ghr` binary.
31	
32	For control plane vs execution plane, lifecycle commands, and how status and logs work, see [Architecture](architecture.md).
33	
34	## Quick start
35	
36	```bash
37	go install github.com/an-lee/ghr/cmd/ghr@latest
38	ghr init
39	# Edit ~/.ghr/runners.yml and ~/.ghr/env (GitHub PAT)
40	ghr doctor
41	ghr setup
42	ghr up
43	ghr status
44	```
45	
46	See [Installation](installation.md) for prerequisites, building from source, and the Makefile. For PAT setup and YAML fields, see [GitHub PAT](github-pat.md) and [Configuration](configuration.md).
47

file.content:
---
title: "ghr"
weight: 1
---

# ghr

**ghr** is a CLI tool to manage self-hosted [GitHub Actions](https://docs.github.com/en/actions) runners across multiple machines from a single control host—typically your laptop—using SSH or local execution.

## Highlights

- **Unified commands** — `up`, `down`, `status`, `setup`, and more work for Linux, macOS, and Windows runners.
- **SSH and local** — Manage remote hosts over SSH, or use `addr: local` to run runners on the same machine as the CLI.
- **Declarative config** — One YAML file lists hosts and runners.
- **Multi-host** — Operate any number of runner machines (desktops, Mac minis, VPS).
- **Docker and native** — Sensible defaults per OS; override with `mode: docker` or `mode: native` where supported.
- **Interactive dashboard** — Run `ghr` on a TTY for a full TUI (same as `ghr dashboard`).

## Architecture (overview)

```text
Your laptop (control plane)
  └── ghr CLI
        ├── local  → This machine (native/Docker runners)
        ├── SSH → Mac mini (native and/or Docker runners)
        ├── SSH → Windows PC (native + Docker runners)
        └── SSH → VPS (Docker runners)
```

The CLI reads your YAML config, connects to each host over SSH (or runs locally for `addr: local`), and runs the right commands to install, start, and stop runner processes. Only the machine where you run **ghr** needs the `ghr` binary.

For control plane vs execution plane, lifecycle commands, and how status and logs work, see [Architecture](architecture.md).

## Quick start

```bash
go install github.com/an-lee/ghr/cmd/ghr@latest
ghr init
# Edit ~/.ghr/runners.yml and ~/.ghr/env (GitHub PAT)
ghr doctor
ghr setup
ghr up
ghr status
```

See [Installation](installation.md) for prerequisites, building from source, and the Makefile. For PAT setup and YAML fields, see [GitHub PAT](github-pat.md) and [Configuration](configuration.md).

content.content:
1	---
2	title: "Architecture"
3	weight: 60
4	---
5	
6	# Architecture
7	
8	This page explains where runners run, how **ghr** reaches each host, and how `status`, `dashboard`, and `logs` collect information.
9	
10	## Control plane vs execution plane
11	
12	**ghr** is a **control plane** only: it runs on your machine and issues commands. The **GitHub Actions runner** (native process or Docker container) always runs on the **target host** from your config—either the same machine as **ghr** when `addr: local`, or a remote machine over SSH.
13	
14	```mermaid
15	flowchart LR
16	  subgraph controlPlane [Control_plane]
17	    ghr[ghr_CLI]
18	  end
19	  subgraph targets [Runner_hosts]
20	    h1[Remote_host_SSH]
21	    h2[Local_addr_local]
22	  end
23	  ghr -->|SSH_session| h1
24	  ghr -->|os_exec| h2
25	  h1 --> n1[Native_runner_or_Docker]
26	  h2 --> n2[Native_runner_or_Docker]
27	```
28	
29	## Where runners run
30	
31	**Mode** (`native` vs `docker`) is resolved per runner. If you omit `mode`, Linux hosts default to **docker**; Windows and macOS default to **native**. You can override with `runners[].mode`.
32	
33	| Host OS | Default mode | Where the workload runs |
34	|--------|--------------|-------------------------|
35	| Linux | `docker` | Docker on that host: container name `gh-runner-<instance>`, image `ghcr.io/actions/actions-runner:latest`. ghr starts it with `config.sh --unattended` (once per container) then `run.sh`, using `ACTIONS_RUNNER_INPUT_*` env vars. On non-Windows hosts the container typically mounts the Docker socket for workflow `docker` steps. |
36	| Linux | `native` (if set) | Files under `~/.ghr/runners/<instance>`; process via `run.sh` and a PID file. |
37	| Windows | `native` | `%USERPROFILE%\.ghr\runners\<instance>`; process via `run.cmd` and a PID file. |
38	| Windows | `docker` (if set) | Same Docker image on Docker Desktop over the same SSH connection as native Windows runners. |
39	| macOS | `native` | `~/.ghr/runners/<instance>`. |
40	| macOS | `docker` (if set) | Same Linux runner image as on Linux; requires Docker Desktop, OrbStack, or Colima with a working `docker` CLI. |
41	
42	**Instances:** `count` creates separate runners named `<name>-1`, `<name>-2`, … on the same host, each with its own directory (native) or container (docker).
43	
44	```mermaid
45	flowchart TB
46	  subgraph linuxHost [Linux_host]
47	    ld[Default_docker]
48	    ln[Explicit_native]
49	    ld --> c1[Container_gh_runner_name_1]
50	    ln --> d1[Dir_HOME_ghr_runners_name_1]
51	  end
52	  subgraph winHost [Windows_host]
53	    wn[Default_native]
54	    wd[Explicit_docker]
55	    wn --> wd1[Dir_USERPROFILE_ghr_runners_name_1]
56	    wd --> wc1[Docker_Desktop_container]
57	  end
58	  subgraph macHost [macOS_host]
59	    mn[Default_native]
60	    md[Explicit_docker]
61	    mn --> md1[Dir_HOME_ghr_runners_name_1]
62	    md --> mc1[Docker_Linux_container]
63	  end
64	```
65	
66	## How commands reach a host
67	
68	- **`addr: local`** — **ghr** runs commands on the machine where the CLI runs (no SSH).
69	- **Remote SSH** — **ghr** opens one SSH client per host for the duration of that subcommand; each remote action uses a new session on that connection.
70	- **Windows over SSH** — Remote automation uses PowerShell (`powershell.exe` or `pwsh.exe` per `windows_ps`) with an encoded command so quoting works regardless of the user’s default SSH shell.
71	- **Linux / macOS over SSH** — Commands run as shell commands on the remote user’s environment.
72	
73	```mermaid
74	sequenceDiagram
75	  participant User
76	  participant Ghr as ghr_CLI
77	  participant Host as Target_host
78	  User->>Ghr: ghr_up_runner_name
79	  Ghr->>Ghr: Load_config_resolve_mode
80	  Ghr->>Host: Connect_SSH_or_local
81	  Ghr->>Host: Start_native_or_docker_per_instance
82	  Host-->>Ghr: OK_or_error
83	```
84	
85	## Lifecycle commands (what they do)
86	
87	| Command | Behavior |
88	|---------|----------|
89	| `ghr setup` | Install/configure runner software or Docker image pull on the host. For **docker** mode on a given host, only the **first** matching runner row runs host-level Docker setup; additional docker-mode runners on that host skip duplicate setup. |
90	| `ghr up` / `ghr down` | Start or stop each instance (native process or container). |
91	| `ghr restart` | `down` then `up`; stop errors are ignored before start. |
92	| `ghr update` | Remove local runner registration (native) or container (docker), then setup and start again—use when upgrading the runner stack. |
93	| `ghr cleanup` | Deletes **offline** runners from the GitHub API only; it does not remove local install dirs or Docker containers. |
94	| `ghr service install` / `uninstall` / `status` | **Native** runners only: install OS autostart (systemd on Linux, LaunchAgent on macOS, scheduled task at logon on Windows). **Docker** rows are skipped; containers already use Docker’s `--restart unless-stopped`. After install, `ghr up` and `ghr down` start/stop the same supervisor instead of a bare `nohup` / Win32 process. |
95	| `ghr doctor` | Read-only checks: local paths, config, GitHub API, SSH targets, Docker vs native prerequisites. Use `--strict` to treat **WARN** as failure. |
96	
97	```mermaid
98	flowchart TD
99	  setup[ghr_setup] --> mode{Mode}
100	  mode -->|docker| dock[Ensure_Docker_and_pull_image]
101	  mode -->|native| nat[Download_configure_actions_runner]
102	  up[ghr_up] --> start[Start_instances]
103	  down[ghr_down] --> stop[Stop_instances]
104	  update[ghr_update] --> remove[Remove_then_setup_then_up]
105	  cleanup[ghr_cleanup] --> api[GitHub_API_delete_offline_only]
106	```
107	
108	## Status, dashboard, and logs
109	
110	**`ghr status`** and **`ghr dashboard`** both:
111	
112	1. Connect to each host (or mark instances **unreachable** if connection fails).
113	2. Ask the host whether each instance is **running** or **stopped** (native: PID file + process check; docker: `docker inspect`).
114	3. Call the **GitHub API** to match runner **names** and the runner’s **OS** (from the API’s `os` field vs native vs docker mode) so two config rows with the same instance name on different platforms do not show each other’s GitHub state.
115	
116	**`ghr logs <name>`** looks up the runner block by **base name** or a full **instance** name (`name-1`, `name-2`, …), connects to the host, then tails logs for that instance. Use **`--host`** when the same instance name exists on more than one machine.
117	
118	- **Native** — Last lines of `runner.log` under that instance’s directory (`~/.ghr/runners/<instance>` on Linux/macOS, or `%USERPROFILE%\.ghr\runners\<instance>` on Windows). On Windows, if that file is missing or empty, ghr tails the newest `*.log` under `_diag` in the same directory.
119	- **Docker** — Last lines from `docker logs` for container `gh-runner-<instance>`.
120	
121	If `count` is greater than 1, pass the specific instance (for example `myapp-1`) so logs match the right directory or container.
122	
123	```mermaid
124	flowchart LR
125	  subgraph localCheck [On_each_host]
126	    L[Process_or_container_state]
127	  end
128	  subgraph remoteApi [GitHub]
129	    A[Runner_list_API]
130	  end
131	  statusCmd[ghr_status_or_dashboard] --> localCheck
132	  statusCmd --> A
133	  localCheck --> table[CLI_or_TUI_table]
134	  A --> table
135	  logsCmd[ghr_logs] --> localCheck
136	  logsCmd --> tail[Log_tail_native_or_docker]
137	```
138	
139	## Autostart and reboot
140	
141	**Docker mode:** `docker run` uses **`--restart unless-stopped`**. If the container was **running** when the host shut down, it is typically started again when the Docker daemon comes up after reboot. **`ghr down`** runs `docker stop`; a **stopped** container is **not** brought back on boot until you run **`ghr up`** (or start the container another way).
142	
143	**Native mode:** A normal **`ghr up`** starts the listener as a background process that **does not** survive reboot. Install autostart once per instance:
144	
145	```bash
146	ghr service install              # all runners (native only; docker rows are skipped)
147	ghr service install myrunner     # one runner block
148	ghr service install --system     # Linux only: unit in /etc/systemd/system (needs passwordless sudo or root SSH)
149	ghr service status
150	ghr service uninstall
151	```
152	
153	**Linux (user units, default):** Units live under `~/.config/systemd/user/`. On many **headless** servers the user manager does not run at boot until someone logs in. Enable **lingering** once (as root): `loginctl enable-linger <ssh-user>` so `systemctl --user` services start at boot.
154	
155	**Linux (`--system`):** Writes `/etc/systemd/system/ghr-runner-<instance>.service` with `User=` / `Group=` set to the SSH user. Requires the same non-interactive **`sudo`** behavior as `ghr setup` on Linux.
156	
157	**macOS:** LaunchAgents run in the **logged-in** user session. A Mac mini without an interactive login may need autologin or another approach for purely headless use.
158	
159	**Windows:** The scheduled task uses an **at logon** trigger (`RunLevel Limited`). Servers that never log on interactively may need a different trigger or service wrapper.
160

file.content:
---
title: "Architecture"
weight: 60
---

# Architecture

This page explains where runners run, how **ghr** reaches each host, and how `status`, `dashboard`, and `logs` collect information.

## Control plane vs execution plane

**ghr** is a **control plane** only: it runs on your machine and issues commands. The **GitHub Actions runner** (native process or Docker container) always runs on the **target host** from your config—either the same machine as **ghr** when `addr: local`, or a remote machine over SSH.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  subgraph controlPlane [Control_plane]
    ghr[ghr_CLI]
  end
  subgraph targets [Runner_hosts]
    h1[Remote_host_SSH]
    h2[Local_addr_local]
  end
  ghr -->|SSH_session| h1
  ghr -->|os_exec| h2
  h1 --> n1[Native_runner_or_Docker]
  h2 --> n2[Native_runner_or_Docker]
```

## Where runners run

**Mode** (`native` vs `docker`) is resolved per runner. If you omit `mode`, Linux hosts default to **docker**; Windows and macOS default to **native**. You can override with `runners[].mode`.

| Host OS | Default mode | Where the workload runs |
|--------|--------------|-------------------------|
| Linux | `docker` | Docker on that host: container name `gh-runner-<instance>`, image `ghcr.io/actions/actions-runner:latest`. ghr starts it with `config.sh --unattended` (once per container) then `run.sh`, using `ACTIONS_RUNNER_INPUT_*` env vars. On non-Windows hosts the container typically mounts the Docker socket for workflow `docker` steps. |
| Linux | `native` (if set) | Files under `~/.ghr/runners/<instance>`; process via `run.sh` and a PID file. |
| Windows | `native` | `%USERPROFILE%\.ghr\runners\<instance>`; process via `run.cmd` and a PID file. |
| Windows | `docker` (if set) | Same Docker image on Docker Desktop over the same SSH connection as native Windows runners. |
| macOS | `native` | `~/.ghr/runners/<instance>`. |
| macOS | `docker` (if set) | Same Linux runner image as on Linux; requires Docker Desktop, OrbStack, or Colima with a working `docker` CLI. |

**Instances:** `count` creates separate runners named `<name>-1`, `<name>-2`, … on the same host, each with its own directory (native) or container (docker).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
  subgraph linuxHost [Linux_host]
    ld[Default_docker]
    ln[Explicit_native]
    ld --> c1[Container_gh_runner_name_1]
    ln --> d1[Dir_HOME_ghr_runners_name_1]
  end
  subgraph winHost [Windows_host]
    wn[Default_native]
    wd[Explicit_docker]
    wn --> wd1[Dir_USERPROFILE_ghr_runners_name_1]
    wd --> wc1[Docker_Desktop_container]
  end
  subgraph macHost [macOS_host]
    mn[Default_native]
    md[Explicit_docker]
    mn --> md1[Dir_HOME_ghr_runners_name_1]
    md --> mc1[Docker_Linux_container]
  end
```

## How commands reach a host

- **`addr: local`** — **ghr** runs commands on the machine where the CLI runs (no SSH).
- **Remote SSH** — **ghr** opens one SSH client per host for the duration of that subcommand; each remote action uses a new session on that connection.
- **Windows over SSH** — Remote automation uses PowerShell (`powershell.exe` or `pwsh.exe` per `windows_ps`) with an encoded command so quoting works regardless of the user’s default SSH shell.
- **Linux / macOS over SSH** — Commands run as shell commands on the remote user’s environment.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
  participant User
  participant Ghr as ghr_CLI
  participant Host as Target_host
  User->>Ghr: ghr_up_runner_name
  Ghr->>Ghr: Load_config_resolve_mode
  Ghr->>Host: Connect_SSH_or_local
  Ghr->>Host: Start_native_or_docker_per_instance
  Host-->>Ghr: OK_or_error
```

## Lifecycle commands (what they do)

| Command | Behavior |
|---------|----------|
| `ghr setup` | Install/configure runner software or Docker image pull on the host. For **docker** mode on a given host, only the **first** matching runner row runs host-level Docker setup; additional docker-mode runners on that host skip duplicate setup. |
| `ghr up` / `ghr down` | Start or stop each instance (native process or container). |
| `ghr restart` | `down` then `up`; stop errors are ignored before start. |
| `ghr update` | Remove local runner registration (native) or container (docker), then setup and start again—use when upgrading the runner stack. |
| `ghr cleanup` | Deletes **offline** runners from the GitHub API only; it does not remove local install dirs or Docker containers. |
| `ghr service install` / `uninstall` / `status` | **Native** runners only: install OS autostart (systemd on Linux, LaunchAgent on macOS, scheduled task at logon on Windows). **Docker** rows are skipped; containers already use Docker’s `--restart unless-stopped`. After install, `ghr up` and `ghr down` start/stop the same supervisor instead of a bare `nohup` / Win32 process. |
| `ghr doctor` | Read-only checks: local paths, config, GitHub API, SSH targets, Docker vs native prerequisites. Use `--strict` to treat **WARN** as failure. |

```mermaid
flowchart TD
  setup[ghr_setup] --> mode{Mode}
  mode -->|docker| dock[Ensure_Docker_and_pull_image]
  mode -->|native| nat[Download_configure_actions_runner]
  up[ghr_up] --> start[Start_instances]
  down[ghr_down] --> stop[Stop_instances]
  update[ghr_update] --> remove[Remove_then_setup_then_up]
  cleanup[ghr_cleanup] --> api[GitHub_API_delete_offline_only]
```

## Status, dashboard, and logs

**`ghr status`** and **`ghr dashboard`** both:

1. Connect to each host (or mark instances **unreachable** if connection fails).
2. Ask the host whether each instance is **running** or **stopped** (native: PID file + process check; docker: `docker inspect`).
3. Call the **GitHub API** to match runner **names** and the runner’s **OS** (from the API’s `os` field vs native vs docker mode) so two config rows with the same instance name on different platforms do not show each other’s GitHub state.

**`ghr logs <name>`** looks up the runner block by **base name** or a full **instance** name (`name-1`, `name-2`, …), connects to the host, then tails logs for that instance. Use **`--host`** when the same instance name exists on more than one machine.

- **Native** — Last lines of `runner.log` under that instance’s directory (`~/.ghr/runners/<instance>` on Linux/macOS, or `%USERPROFILE%\.ghr\runners\<instance>` on Windows). On Windows, if that file is missing or empty, ghr tails the newest `*.log` under `_diag` in the same directory.
- **Docker** — Last lines from `docker logs` for container `gh-runner-<instance>`.

If `count` is greater than 1, pass the specific instance (for example `myapp-1`) so logs match the right directory or container.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
  subgraph localCheck [On_each_host]
    L[Process_or_container_state]
  end
  subgraph remoteApi [GitHub]
    A[Runner_list_API]
  end
  statusCmd[ghr_status_or_dashboard] --> localCheck
  statusCmd --> A
  localCheck --> table[CLI_or_TUI_table]
  A --> table
  logsCmd[ghr_logs] --> localCheck
  logsCmd --> tail[Log_tail_native_or_docker]
```

## Autostart and reboot

**Docker mode:** `docker run` uses **`--restart unless-stopped`**. If the container was **running** when the host shut down, it is typically started again when the Docker daemon comes up after reboot. **`ghr down`** runs `docker stop`; a **stopped** container is **not** brought back on boot until you run **`ghr up`** (or start the container another way).

**Native mode:** A normal **`ghr up`** starts the listener as a background process that **does not** survive reboot. Install autostart once per instance:

```bash
ghr service install              # all runners (native only; docker rows are skipped)
ghr service install myrunner     # one runner block
ghr service install --system     # Linux only: unit in /etc/systemd/system (needs passwordless sudo or root SSH)
ghr service status
ghr service uninstall
```

**Linux (user units, default):** Units live under `~/.config/systemd/user/`. On many **headless** servers the user manager does not run at boot until someone logs in. Enable **lingering** once (as root): `loginctl enable-linger <ssh-user>` so `systemctl --user` services start at boot.

**Linux (`--system`):** Writes `/etc/systemd/system/ghr-runner-<instance>.service` with `User=` / `Group=` set to the SSH user. Requires the same non-interactive **`sudo`** behavior as `ghr setup` on Linux.

**macOS:** LaunchAgents run in the **logged-in** user session. A Mac mini without an interactive login may need autologin or another approach for purely headless use.

**Windows:** The scheduled task uses an **at logon** trigger (`RunLevel Limited`). Servers that never log on interactively may need a different trigger or service wrapper.

file.content:
# ghr

**ghr** is a CLI for managing self-hosted [GitHub Actions](https://docs.github.com/en/actions) runners across many machines from one place—usually your laptop. It connects over **SSH** (or runs **locally** with `addr: local`), installs and starts runners, and gives you a **terminal UI** for day-to-day operations.

**Repository:** [github.com/an-lee/ghr](https://github.com/an-lee/ghr)  
**Go module:** `github.com/an-lee/ghr` (used by `go install` and imports).

## Features

- One set of commands for **Linux**, **macOS**, and **Windows** runners (`setup`, `up`, `down`, `status`, `logs`, `service`, …).
- **Declarative YAML** for hosts and runners; secrets in `~/.ghr/env`.
- **Docker or native** runners per row, with sensible OS defaults and overrides.
- **Multi-host** from a single config; optional **TUI** dashboard (`ghr` or `ghr dashboard` on a TTY).

## Documentation

**Full documentation (user guide, architecture, config reference):**  
[https://an-lee.github.io/ghr/](https://an-lee.github.io/ghr/)

The Markdown sources live under [`docs/`](docs/) in this repo and are built with [Hugo](https://gohugo.io/) (Hugo Book theme).

## Install

```bash
go install github.com/an-lee/ghr/cmd/ghr@latest
```

Requires a recent **Go** toolchain (see `go.mod` / CI for the exact version).

## Quick start

```bash
ghr init
# Edit ~/.ghr/runners.yml and ~/.ghr/env (GitHub PAT — see docs)
ghr doctor
ghr setup
ghr up
ghr status
```

Then run `ghr` on a terminal for the interactive dashboard, or use `ghr status`, `ghr logs <name>`, etc.

## Development

```bash
make check    # vet + tests (same idea as CI)
# or: go vet ./... && go test ./... -race -count=1
```

Repository layout is described in [docs/reference/file-structure.md](docs/reference/file-structure.md).

## Publishing documentation (maintainers)

Documentation deploys to GitHub Pages via **GitHub Actions** when changes land on `main`. In the GitHub repo, enable **Settings → Pages → Build and deployment → Source: GitHub Actions** once (no branch-based `/docs` hosting required for the built site).

Local preview:

```bash
# from the repository root:
git clone https://github.com/alex-shpak/hugo-book docs/themes/book --depth 1
cd docs && hugo server
```

Build (also run in CI):

```bash
cd docs && hugo --minify
```

# Plan: Add Introduction Page to ghr Documentation

## Context

The ghr documentation at `docs/content/` currently has a brief intro embedded in `_index.md` (the home page). It covers highlights, a quick architecture diagram, and quick start commands — but it's thin on explaining *what problem ghr solves* and *how it actually works* at a conceptual level. Users need a clear introduction before diving into installation or configuration.

## What to Create

A new `docs/content/introduction.md` that serves as the primary introduction page, explaining:

1. **What is ghr?** — The problem: managing self-hosted runners across many machines is tedious
2. **What does ghr do?** — CLI tool that automates setup, lifecycle (up/down/restart), and monitoring of GitHub Actions runners on remote machines
3. **How does it work?** — Control plane over SSH (or local execution), runner software installed on target hosts, YAML config drives everything
4. **Core concepts** — Config file, hosts, runners, modes (docker vs native), instances

## Decision Needed

**Where should the introduction live?**

Option A: **Replace `_index.md` content** — Keep `_index.md` as the intro, move existing quick-start/architecture deeper (not ideal — `_index.md` weight=1 is already acting as landing page)

Option B: **New `introduction.md` as weight 2** — Insert before the current `_index.md` content. `_index.md` remains the very brief landing page with just highlights + quick start, while `introduction.md` becomes the full conceptual intro. This is the recommended approach.

**Key question before proceeding:**
- Should the new `introduction.md` replace the "Highlights" and "Architecture (overview)" sections currently in `_index.md`, or should it be entirely new content that doesn't touch `_index.md`?

## Files to Create/Modify

- **Create:** `docs/content/introduction.md` — Full introduction page
- **Modify:** `docs/content/_index.md` — Possibly trim to keep it a brief landing page (depending on answer above)

## Content Outline for `introduction.md`

```
---
title: "Introduction"
weight: 2
---

# Introduction

## The Problem
Managing self-hosted GitHub Actions runners across multiple machines is tedious: SSH into each machine, download the runner, configure, start, monitor...

## What is ghr?
ghr is a CLI control plane that manages runner lifecycle on remote machines from a single host (your laptop). You write YAML, ghr handles the rest.

## How It Works
1. You define hosts and runners in `~/.ghr/runners.yml`
2. ghr connects to each host over SSH (or runs locally)
3. For each runner instance, ghr installs runner software and manages the process/container
4. Status, logs, and dashboard commands pull state from both the host and GitHub API

## Key Concepts
- **Control plane vs execution plane**
- **Hosts** — machines where runners run
- **Runners** — instances defined in YAML; can be docker or native
- **Config** — `~/.ghr/runners.yml` (hosts, runners, mode, count)
- **Secrets** — GitHub PAT stored in `~/.ghr/env`
```

## Verification

- [ ] `docs/content/introduction.md` created with meaningful content
- [ ] Hugo renders the new page correctly (`hugo server` locally or CI check)
- [ ] Navigation order is sensible (intro before installation)
- [ ] Links from `_index.md` reference the new introduction page if content was moved

## Context

The ghr documentation has a brief intro embedded in `_index.md` covering highlights and a quick architecture diagram, but no dedicated page explaining what ghr is, what problem it solves, and how it works conceptually. A new page at weight 2 (after `_index.md` which is weight 1) covering:

1. **The Problem** — Managing self-hosted runners across many machines is tedious (SSH, download, configure, start per machine)
2. **What is ghr?** — A CLI control plane that manages runner lifecycle on remote machines from a single host (your laptop)

- Hugo renders both pages correctly
- Page order: `_index.md` (weight 1) → `introduction.md` (weight 2) → `installation.md` (weight 10) → ...

input.content:
---
title: "Introduction"
weight: 2
---

# Introduction

## The Problem

Self-hosted GitHub Actions runners give you more control over your CI/CD infrastructure — but managing them across multiple machines is tedious. To add a new runner you must SSH into each machine, download the runner software, configure it, start the process, and remember which machine has what. Monitoring, updating, and restarting runners means touching every machine individually. As the number of runners and machines grows, this overhead becomes unmanageable.

## What is ghr?

**ghr** is a CLI control plane that manages self-hosted GitHub Actions runners on remote machines from a single host — typically your laptop. Instead of SSHing into each machine, you describe your infrastructure in a YAML file and run commands like `ghr setup`, `ghr up`, and `ghr status` to manage everything from one place.

ghr handles:

- **Installation** — Downloads and configures the GitHub Actions runner software on each host
- **Lifecycle** — Starts, stops, and restarts runner instances (as native processes or Docker containers)
- **Monitoring** — Pulls real-time status and logs from each host and the GitHub API
- **Autostart** — Installs system services so runners survive reboots

## How It Works

```
Your laptop (control plane)          Runner hosts
                                    ──────────────
  ghr CLI ──────────────────────────►  Mac mini
        │                              Linux VPS
        │                              Windows PC
        │                              localhost
        │
        ▼
  ~/.ghr/runners.yml  (your config)
  ~/.ghr/env          (GitHub PAT)
```

1. **Config** — You describe hosts and runners in `~/.ghr/runners.yml`
2. **Connect** — ghr reaches each host over SSH (or runs locally with `addr: local`)
3. **Install** — `ghr setup` downloads the runner software, configures it with your GitHub PAT, and (for Docker mode) pulls the runner image
4. **Manage** — `ghr up` / `ghr down` start and stop runner instances as processes or containers
5. **Monitor** — `ghr status` and `ghr dashboard` show running state from each host alongside GitHub's online/offline view

Only the machine where ghr runs needs the ghr binary. Target hosts only need SSH access and (for Docker mode) a working Docker installation.

## Key Concepts

### Control plane vs execution plane

ghr is a **control plane only**. The ghr CLI runs on your machine and issues commands. The actual runner process or container always runs on the **target host** defined in your config — not on the machine where ghr executes.

### Hosts

A **host** is a machine (physical or virtual) where runners run. You specify it in `runners.yml` as an address — either `addr: local` to run runners on the same machine as ghr, or `user@hostname` for an SSH connection.

### Runners

A **runner** is a declared runner instance in your config. Each runner has a name, a target host, an OS type, and a mode. You can run multiple instances of the same runner with `count` — for example, `count: 4` creates `myrunner-1`, `myrunner-2`, `myrunner-3`, and `myrunner-4` on the same host.

### Docker vs native mode

Runners can run as **native** processes or inside **Docker** containers:

- **native** — Runner software installed directly on the host filesystem; process managed by a shell script and PID file
- **docker** — Runner runs in a container (`ghcr.io/actions/actions-runner:latest`); container managed by Docker with `--restart unless-stopped`

Linux defaults to **docker** if you don't specify. Windows and macOS default to **native**.

### Config and secrets

- **`~/.ghr/runners.yml`** — Declares all hosts and runners (OS, mode, count, labels, etc.)
- **`~/.ghr/env`** — Contains environment variables, most importantly your GitHub PAT (`GHR_TOKEN`)

### Instances

When `count` is greater than 1, ghr creates multiple instances of a runner on the same host, each with its own directory (native) or container (docker). Instances are named `<runner-name>-1`, `<runner-name>-2`, and so on.

toolUseResult.content:
---
title: "Introduction"
weight: 2
---

# Introduction

## The Problem

Self-hosted GitHub Actions runners give you more control over your CI/CD infrastructure — but managing them across multiple machines is tedious. To add a new runner you must SSH into each machine, download the runner software, configure it, start the process, and remember which machine has what. Monitoring, updating, and restarting runners means touching every machine individually. As the number of runners and machines grows, this overhead becomes unmanageable.

## What is ghr?

**ghr** is a CLI control plane that manages self-hosted GitHub Actions runners on remote machines from a single host — typically your laptop. Instead of SSHing into each machine, you describe your infrastructure in a YAML file and run commands like `ghr setup`, `ghr up`, and `ghr status` to manage everything from one place.

ghr handles:

- **Installation** — Downloads and configures the GitHub Actions runner software on each host
- **Lifecycle** — Starts, stops, and restarts runner instances (as native processes or Docker containers)
- **Monitoring** — Pulls real-time status and logs from each host and the GitHub API
- **Autostart** — Installs system services so runners survive reboots

## How It Works

```
Your laptop (control plane)          Runner hosts
                                    ──────────────
  ghr CLI ──────────────────────────►  Mac mini
        │                              Linux VPS
        │                              Windows PC
        │                              localhost
        │
        ▼
  ~/.ghr/runners.yml  (your config)
  ~/.ghr/env          (GitHub PAT)
```

1. **Config** — You describe hosts and runners in `~/.ghr/runners.yml`
2. **Connect** — ghr reaches each host over SSH (or runs locally with `addr: local`)
3. **Install** — `ghr setup` downloads the runner software, configures it with your GitHub PAT, and (for Docker mode) pulls the runner image
4. **Manage** — `ghr up` / `ghr down` start and stop runner instances as processes or containers
5. **Monitor** — `ghr status` and `ghr dashboard` show running state from each host alongside GitHub's online/offline view

Only the machine where ghr runs needs the ghr binary. Target hosts only need SSH access and (for Docker mode) a working Docker installation.

## Key Concepts

### Control plane vs execution plane

ghr is a **control plane only**. The ghr CLI runs on your machine and issues commands. The actual runner process or container always runs on the **target host** defined in your config — not on the machine where ghr executes.

### Hosts

A **host** is a machine (physical or virtual) where runners run. You specify it in `runners.yml` as an address — either `addr: local` to run runners on the same machine as ghr, or `user@hostname` for an SSH connection.

### Runners

A **runner** is a declared runner instance in your config. Each runner has a name, a target host, an OS type, and a mode. You can run multiple instances of the same runner with `count` — for example, `count: 4` creates `myrunner-1`, `myrunner-2`, `myrunner-3`, and `myrunner-4` on the same host.

### Docker vs native mode

Runners can run as **native** processes or inside **Docker** containers:

- **native** — Runner software installed directly on the host filesystem; process managed by a shell script and PID file
- **docker** — Runner runs in a container (`ghcr.io/actions/actions-runner:latest`); container managed by Docker with `--restart unless-stopped`

Linux defaults to **docker** if you don't specify. Windows and macOS default to **native**.

### Config and secrets

- **`~/.ghr/runners.yml`** — Declares all hosts and runners (OS, mode, count, labels, etc.)
- **`~/.ghr/env`** — Contains environment variables, most importantly your GitHub PAT (`GHR_TOKEN`)

### Instances

When `count` is greater than 1, ghr creates multiple instances of a runner on the same host, each with its own directory (native) or container (docker). Instances are named `<runner-name>-1`, `<runner-name>-2`, and so on.
